Specifically, the proposed order addresses the use of confidential court data with generative artificial intelligence.\n\nThe court adopted a previously proposed rule warning of the risks associated with uploading confidential or sealed information into generative artificial intelligence tools, stating that doing so may violate existing court rules governing document handling and professional conduct.\n\nThe order came into effect alongside a case where the Arkansas Supreme Court ordered an attorney to show cause why she should not be sanctioned for using artificial intelligence in preparing a petition filed with the court. The court expressed concerns that citations may have been the result of generative artificial intelligence tools and thus 'hallucinated'.\n\nThe full text of the administrative order appears to be available through legal databases, with the case citation 2025 Ark. 117."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"05b50e88-b454-41e9-8e9d-ba5c0315e20c"}

Here are the key findings:\n\n## Arizona Supreme Court Technology Competence Rule (2025)\n\nThe Arizona Supreme Court adopted a judicial duty of technology competence on August 27, 2025, adding the ethical obligation of technology competence to the state's judicial ethics code.(no content)This decision to explicitly insert a technology competence requirement into its rules for judges is a first in the nation.(no content)Effective January 1, 2026, Rule 2.5 of the Arizona Code of Judicial Conduct includes new Comment 1, which reads: \"Competence in the performance of judicial duties requires the legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness, and preparation reasonably necessary to perform a judge's responsibilities of judicial office, including the use of, and knowledge of the benefits and risks associated with, technology relevant to service as a judicial officer.\"(no content)The need to cope with and ethically deploy artificial intelligence technologies appears to be the leading motivation for the rules change.\n\n## Arizona Steering Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts\n\nA proposal to explicitly recognize a duty of technology competence for judges was raised in \"Generative AI: Ethical Best Practices for Lawyers and Judges,\" written by the Arizona Supreme Court's Steering Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts.(no content)The steering committee filed a petition to make the change with the Arizona high court in 2024.\n\nThe State Bar of Arizona has also issued guidance emphasizing that legal professionals must align AI use with their professional responsibilities under the Arizona Rules of Professional Conduct and Arizona Supreme Court Rules."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"c2548fe9-cd7e-4465-98b2-c7d604e56ded"}

Here are the key findings:\n\n## California Judicial Council AI Task Force and Rules\n\nThe California Judicial Council Artificial Intelligence Task Force was established by California Supreme Court Chief Justice Patricia Guerrero in May 2024 to evaluate and develop policy recommendations for the use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) in the state's judicial branch.(no content)On July 18, 2025, the Judicial Council adopted Rule 10.430 and Standard 10.80, effective September 1, 2025.\n\n## Rule 10.430: Generative AI Use Policies\n\nRule 10.430, Generative Artificial Intelligence Use Policies, applies to the California Superior Courts, Courts of Appeal, and Supreme Court. It requires that any court that permits the use of generative AI by its staff or judicial officers must adopt a use policy by December 15, 2025.(no content)The accompanying Standard 10.80 functions as a set of guidelines for policy drafting. Each court may either adopt the guidelines or draft a policy of its own reflecting the content and scope of the rule.\n\n## Key Principles\n\nCalifornia's court system, the largest in the U.S. with 65 courts, 1,800 judges, and five million cases annually, is the first to adopt such comprehensive AI rules, setting a national standard.\n\nThe rule outlines six guiding principles, including prohibitions on entering confidential information into public AI tools and prohibitions on using AI to discriminate based on protected classes.\n\nCourts may entirely prohibit the use of generative AI and may also set restrictions on how generative AI may be used for court-related work, such as allowing or prohibiting the use of specific generative AI tools, allowing use of generative AI only for particular tasks, or requiring approval for the use of generative AI."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"387936f4-551a-46cb-bec3-bd34cc00aba0"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_017c6PqnQy1KpfQ7x7hiMQZx","type":"tool_result","content":"    <title>Rule 10.430.  Generative artificial intelligence use policies | Judicial Branch of California</title>\n<span>Rule 10.430.  Generative artificial intelligence use policies</span>\n<p class=\"paragraphlist\"><span><span><span><span>(2)  “Generative artificial intelligence” or “generative AI” means a computer-based system that uses machine learning or similar techniques to produce new content—such as text, images, audio, video, code, or data visualizations—in response to user inputs. Generative AI systems create content that is not pre-programmed or explicitly retrieved but synthesized based on underlying models trained on large datasets and may include integration with other sources, such as real-time access to proprietary databases.</span></span></span></span></p>\n<p class=\"paragraphlist\"><span><span><span><span>(1)  Prohibit the entry of confidential, personal identifying, or other nonpublic information into a public generative AI system. Personal identifying information includes driver’s license numbers; dates of birth; Social Security numbers; National Crime Information and Criminal Identification and Information numbers; addresses and phone numbers of parties, victims, witnesses, and court personnel; medical or psychiatric information; financial information; account numbers; and any other content sealed by court order or deemed confidential by court rule or statute.</span></span></span></span></p>\n<p class=\"rulehist\"><span><span><span><span>Rule 10.430 adopted effective September 1, 2025.</span></span></span></span></p>\n<p class=\"advcommtext\"><span><span><span><strong>Subdivision (d).</strong> This subdivision does not require any court to permit the use of generative AI by court staff or judicial officers. Courts may entirely prohibit the use of generative AI and may also set restrictions on how generative AI may be used for court-related work, such as allowing or prohibiting the use of specific generative AI tools, allowing use of generative AI only for particular tasks, or requiring approval for the use of generative AI. Courts that are required by subdivision (b) to adopt a use policy because they are not prohibiting the use of generative AI for court-related work can comply with subdivision (d) by adopting verbatim the nonoptional sections of the <em>Model Policy for Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence</em>, or by adopting a policy that uses substantially similar language. Courts adopting a generative AI use policy under this rule may make their policy more restrictive than the rule requires and may include provisions not covered by rule 10.430. </span></span></span></p>","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"24332cbe-b163-427b-b5d2-e04e5d32f6a2"}

Here's what the search revealed:\n\n## Illinois Supreme Court AI Policy (Effective January 1, 2025)\n\nThe Illinois Supreme Court announced the release of its policy on Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the courts in December 2024, following the approval of a report submitted by the Illinois Judicial Conference (IJC) Task Force on Artificial Intelligence.\n\n### Key Policy Provisions\n\nThe use of AI by litigants, attorneys, judges, judicial clerks, research attorneys, and court staff is authorized provided it complies with legal and ethical standards, and disclosure of AI use should not be required in a pleading.(no content)The Rules of Professional Conduct and the Code of Judicial Conduct apply fully to the use of AI technologies.\n\n### Ethical Requirements\n\nAll users must thoroughly review AI-generated content before submitting it in any court proceeding to ensure accuracy and compliance with legal and ethical obligations.(no content)The Illinois Supreme Court remains committed to upholding the highest ethical standards while acknowledging that AI raises critical concerns about authenticity, accuracy, bias, and the integrity of court filings, proceedings, evidence, and decisions.\n\n### Additional Guidance\n\nIn October 2025, the Illinois Attorney Registration & Disciplinary Commission (ARDC) released The Illinois Attorney's Guide to Implementing AI, which provides attorneys with background information on AI tools, discusses their risks, and offers strategies for ethically and effectively integrating AI tools into the practice of law.\n\nThe policy emphasizes that while AI use is encouraged for efficiency, legal professionals remain fully accountable for their work product and must ensure compliance with existing professional standards."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"2cebc92d-97d1-47d0-a537-ca7103b845f0"}

in Chief Justice Kimberly Budd's State of the Judiciary speech in December 2025.\n\n### Key Guidelines:\n\n- GenAI is currently only used for administrative tasks, with only public information allowed and AI tools cannot retain or use any entered information to train the programs\n\n- The guidelines are a \"modest first step\" and are expected to be updated as the judiciary learns more about GenAI\n\n- The court recognizes that GenAI offers enormous potential for streamlining many aspects of the work of attorneys and courts alike\n\n## Attorney General's AI Advisory (April Misuse\n\nIn 2025, a lawyer who submitted court filings that included hallucinated fictitious legal authorities was sanctioned, with the court imposing mandatory legal education training\n\nThe Massachusetts judiciary is taking a careful, incremental approach to AI adoption, focusing on administrative uses while developing appropriate ethical The committee was established in August 2024 and was charged with assessing the risks and benefits associated with the use of Generative AI in the courts and making recommendations to help maintain public trust and confidence in the judicial system.(no content)Chief Justice Michael P. Boggs established the committee on October 22, 2024, in partnership with the National Center for State Courts (NCSC).(no content)The committee is chaired by Justice Andrew A. Pinson.\n\n## Committee's Focus Areas\n\nThe Georgia initiative examines a wide range of topics, including: the impact of artificial intelligence on evidence rules, the impact on civil and criminal procedure rules, the adequacy of current ethical and professional standards applicable to lawyer competency relating to the use of AI by lawyers practicing in Georgia courts, and the need for standards and benchmarks for technology vendors to improve AI performance and ensure transparency and privacy.\n\n## State Bar Collaboration\n\nIn coordination with the Supreme Court, the State Bar of Georgia also has a committee, chaired by attorney Darrell Sutton, that is reviewing how AI impacts the Georgia Rules of Professional Conduct and other practice-of-law-related matters involving AI.\n\nThe search results show that Georgia has been actively developing AI policies for its court system throughout 2024 and into 2025, with the formal report being submitted in July 2025."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"139f36d7-a1e3-451e-ba64-1d9eca6a8d67"}

In this effort, the Committee conducted a comparative review of AI-related initiatives, policy frameworks, and draft guidelines from other jurisdictions to inform the development of Pennsylvania's approach.\n\nThe Supreme Court also issued General Ethics Guidance No. 2-2025 to accompany the policy, providing ethical guidance for judicial officers on using AI technology in accordance with the Pennsylvania Code of Judicial Conduct."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"4c6684e5-24cd-4a2e-9c17-00d23e9aee6e"}

its very first official policy on the use of artificial intelligence (\"AI\") in the courts.(no content)This interim policy on the use of artificial intelligence is designed to promote the responsible and ethical use of AI technology in the New York State Unified Court System, outlining important guardrails to ensure fairness, accountability, and security in the use of AI, particularly generative AI, by the workforce.\n\n### Scope and Application\n\nThe interim policy is applicable to all judges and nonjudicial employees of the UCS, and it applies to all functions performed on a UCS-owned device, and to all UCS-related work performed on any device.\n\n### Key Guidance Points\n\nThe UCS stresses that AI is only meant to be an assistant and \"must not be treated as a substitute for human judgment, discretion, or decision-making.\" The policy requires that all AI outputs must be reviewed by users to correct any biased or inaccurate content, and strictly prohibits the disclosure of confidential information to public AI platforms.\n\nThe policy acknowledges that despite its potential benefits, generative AI can produce inaccurate, wholly fabricated, or biased outputs, and can jeopardize the security of data entered into the program.\n\n### Advisory Committee\n\nThe Advisory Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts was established in June 2024.(no content)The committee has released its inaugural annual report, a landmark document outlining policies, recommendations, and ethical frameworks for the responsible use of AI in the state's court Here's a summary of the key findings:\n\n## Texas AI Legislation and Court Policies (2024-2025)\n\n### Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (TRAIGA)\n\nOn June 22, 2025, Texas Governor Greg Abbott signed the Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (HB 149) into law, which will go into effect on January 1, 2026.(no content)This marks Texas as the second U.S. state, after Colorado, to enact a comprehensive AI law.(no content)Originally introduced in late 2024 as HB 1709, TRAIGA in its final form significantly reduces the law's regulatory scheme—eliminating most of the private sector obligations and focusing on government agencies' use of AI systems and the use of AI for certain limited purposes.\n\nKey features include:\n- Prohibits AI systems designed for \"restricted purposes\" including encouragement of self-harm, unlawful discrimination, constitutional rights infringement, and CSAM generation.\n- The Act prohibits intentional discrimination using AI systems, but disparate impact alone is not sufficient to show unlawful behavior.\n- TRAIGA provides for an AI Regulatory Sandbox, allowing companies to test AI systems under relaxed regulatory oversight for up to 36 months. This initiative is intended to foster innovation while still maintaining consumer protections.\n\n### Texas Bar and Court Guidance\n\nThe Professional Ethics Committee for the State Bar of Texas recently released Opinion 705 to address how Texas attorneys can ethically use generative artificial intelligence in their practice. Opinion 705 clarifies the ethical considerations lawyers must address to leverage these tools safely and effectively.(no content)Lawyers are responsible for the work product they submit regardless of who (or what) does the original research and drafting. That means lawyers cannot blindly rely upon or use answers given by generative AI tools.\n\n### Texas Court Orders on AI Use\n\nAt least one federal judge in Texas has set strict standards for AI in the courtroom. The judge requires parties to confirm that they have either not filed any documents written by AI or that a human has thoroughly checked any AI-generated filings for accuracy and sound legal reasoning.(no content)Texas state courts, such as the 394th District Court, are also beginning to set AI Certification policies.(no content)One Texas court has required that all filers (including self-represented litigants) certify that \"all language, quotations, sources, citations, arguments, and legal analysis created or contributed to by generative artificial intelligence were before submission verified as accurate through traditional (non-AI) legal sources by an attorney licensed to practice law in the State of Texas.\""}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"6eee1c55-3226-4bb3-9066-5748fca203c3"}

The Court created an AI Governance Committee that has worked in multiple phases:\n\n### Internal Policy\nThe Indiana Supreme Court formed an AI Governance Committee whose first charge was to develop an internal AI use policy for the Supreme Court and the Office of Judicial Administration.(no content)The final policy, approved by the Supreme Court, encourages responsible use of AI to support employees' work while reinforcing the importance of adhering to ethical rules.\n\n### Guidance for Trial Courts\nLast fall, the Court released resources through its AI Governance Committee for local courts to develop AI policies.(no content)The bench card, checklist, model policies and buyer's guide provided to trial courts serve as beginner, intermediate and advanced levels of AI adoption.\n\n### Key Principles\nThe AI Governance Committee's bench card includes the key principles for court use of AI: transparency, accountability, awareness and confidentiality.(no content)The policy also highlights the need for vigilance and adherence to ethical standards when using AI, particularly in recognizing the potential bias and inaccuracies that may be embedded in AI-generated content.(no content)The policy draws a distinction between AI software with open models and closed models, alerting employees that open models make any entered information available to all other users of the platform. Toolkit: AI Governance Committee Delivers Model Policies for Indiana Trial Courts</div><article class=\"post-9603 post type-post status-publish format-standard has-post-thumbnail category-features entry\" aria-label=\"From Guardrails to Toolkit: AI Governance Committee Delivers Model Policies for Indiana Trial Courts\" itemscope itemtype=\"https://schema.org/CreativeWork\"><header class=\"entry-header\"><h1 class=\"entry-title\" itemprop=\"headline\">From Guardrails to Toolkit: AI Governance Committee Delivers Model Policies for Indiana Trial Courts</h1>\n<p>In the Summer issue of the Court Times, I wrote about the Supreme Court’s creation of an AI Governance Committee to develop <a href=\"https://times.courts.in.gov/2025/08/06/guardrails-for-the-future/\" data-type=\"post\" data-id=\"9534\">internal guardrails around AI use</a> in response to the rise of AI in the courts. At that time, the Court had approved and distributed a foundational policy to its staff to ensure responsible use of AI that complied with Court employees’ ethical duties.</p>\n<p>The model policies have been approved by the Supreme Court and were circulated to trial courts via the Weekly Bulletin. They are also posted on INcite under the Benchbook application. The committee’s goal is that trial court judges use these tools to build their own policies that meet the needs of their courts.</p>\n<p>Promulgating model policies for trial courts in Indiana presents specific challenges because of our non-unified structure. County-based trial courts have a varied makeup of judicial officers, caseloads, staffing, and resources; all of these factors are relevant when building an AI use policy. To the committee, this meant a flexible, modular approach to a model use policy was necessary.</p>","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"a1a1bf7b-f2b6-420d-938c-5070a9bb8221"}

This opinion addresses how lawyers can bill for work done using AI tools and discusses value-based billing approaches.\n\n## Criminal Justice AI Restrictions\n\nVirginia has specific statutory requirements for AI use in criminal proceedings. No decisions related to criminal matters shall be made without the involvement of a human decision-maker, and the use of any recommendation or prediction from an artificial intelligence-based tool shall be subject to any challenge or objection permitted by law.\n\n## General Requirements for Attorneys\n\nWhile Virginia law does not explicitly prohibit the use of AI tools for legal research or drafting pleadings, attorneys must ensure compliance with existing rules regarding the accuracy, good faith, and proper purpose of their submissions, and local and judge-specific rules may impose further requirements, such as disclosure or certification, when AI tools are utilized.\n\nThe official Virginia Courts website (courts.state.va.us) has a dedicated page for AI policy information, and the full ethics opinion can be viewed on the Supreme Court of Virginia's website, vacourts.gov."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"b8298748-43ed-4cc2-933e-afccf5567386"}

Mezu, No. 316, September Term 2025 (October 29, 2025), resulting in a referral to the Attorney Grievance Commission.(no content)The court reported this case to address a problem recurring in courts around the country: the use of AI to draft briefs or other pleadings, resulting in incorrect, inaccurate, or fictitious case citations.\n\n## Maryland Court's Position on AI Use\n\nThe case appears to be the first in which a Maryland appeals court has addressed issues that take place when lawyers use AI without exercising due diligence.(no content)The Maryland Appellate Court argued that \"the signature of an attorney on a pleading or paper constitutes a certification that the attorney has read the pleading or paper; that to the best of the attorney's knowledge, information, and belief there is good ground to support it.\"\n\n## Legislative Response\n\nMaryland is establishing an Artificial Intelligence Evidence Clinic Pilot Program within the Administrative Office of the Courts to provide expertise in artificial intelligence to the circuit courts and the District Court in the form of expert testimony.(no content)This incident is at least the third time in 2025 that a Maryland attorney has been accused of using AI-fabricated material in legal briefs, and lawyers who misuse AI may be subject to penalties from the judge overseeing their case or discipline by the state's Attorney Grievance Commission."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"ba9a2564-2cf3-43cc-963a-64858af93aeb"}

Here's what I learned:\n\n## South Carolina Supreme Court AI Policy\n\nOn March 25, 2025, Chief Justice Kittredge issued an interim policy providing guidance on the appropriate use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (\"AI\") tools in the South Carolina Judicial Branch.\n\n### Key Provisions:\n\n**Application and Definitions:**\nThe Interim Policy applies to all Judicial Officers and Employees of the South Carolina Judicial Branch, including Justices, judges, attorneys, law clerks, administrative assistants, interns, externs, temporary employees, paralegals, and all other employees or volunteers within the Branch.\n\n**Approved Use Only:**\nJudicial Branch staffers can only use generative AI tools and systems in the performance of their duties that are approved by the court or the South Carolina Court Administration.\n\n**Human Oversight Required:**\nGenerative AI cannot be used to draft memoranda, orders, opinions or other documents without direct human oversight and approval.(no content)Content from Generative AI may not be used verbatim; be assumed to be truthful, reliable, or accurate; be treated as the sole source of reference; or be solely relied on in making final decisions.\n\n**Confidentiality Protection:**\nNeither AI or generative AI are permitted to be used to process or analyze confidential court records or privileged information and communications unless expressly authorized.\n\n**Guidance for Attorneys:**\nLawyers and litigants are responsible for ensuring the accuracy of all generative AI-produced documents and must ensure that generative AI does not compromise client confidentiality or the South Carolina Rules of Professional Conduct.(no content)South Carolina is among at least 10 states where state judges have issued an order or guidance around the use of artificial intelligence.(no content)The interim policy will remain in effect indefinitely, undergoing \"ongoing\" evaluation and future policy development until the South Carolina Supreme Court or Chief Justice Kitteredge order otherwise."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"cca42a93-aa16-4cbb-8226-881f45a53219"}

Let me provide you with what I've found:\n\n## Search Results Summary\n\nThe search for \"Oregon Supreme Court AI policy artificial intelligence\" revealed several relevant AI policies and guidance related to Oregon, though not a specific Oregon Supreme Court policy for judicial use:\n\n### Oregon State Bar Formal Opinion on AI (2025-205)\n\nIn February 2025, the Oregon State Bar issued Formal Opinion No. 2025-205, becoming one of the first jurisdictions to codify expectations for AI-assisted filings.(no content)Oregon's opinion does not ban AI. It insists on supervision. Lawyers may use generative models, but they must review and verify every citation, quotation, and factual statement the tool produces before filing.\n\nThe opinion addresses various professional responsibilities:\n- Lawyers must verify any work done by or with the assistance of any third-party technology, including AI.\n- If lawyers would like to use their clients' information in an open model, lawyers must obtain their clients' informed consent. \"'[I]nformed consent' denotes the agreement by a person to a proposed course of conduct after the lawyer has communicated adequate information and explanation about the material risks of and reasonably available alternatives to the proposed course of conduct.\"\n\n### Oregon Attorney General's AI Guidance (December 2024)\n\nOn December 24, 2024, the Oregon Attorney General published AI guidance, \"What you should know about how Oregon's laws may affect your company's use of Artificial Intelligence,\" (the \"Guidance\") that clarifies how existing Oregon consumer protection, privacy and anti-discrimination laws apply to AI tools.\n\nThe guidance emphasizes that several existing Oregon laws apply to AI:\n- The Oregon Unlawful Trade Practices Act (UTPA)\n- The Oregon Consumer Privacy Act (OCPA)\n- The Oregon Equality Act\n- The Oregon Consumer Information Protection Act\n\n### Oregon Legislation on AI\n\n**AI Companion Law (SB 1546):**\nIn Oregon's 2026 \"short session,\" legislators passed a targeted AI companion bill (SB 1546) promoted by a coalition of suicide prevention and mental health advocates. Despite being only three pages long, the bill packs a punch, regulating AI chatbots primarily as a consumer protection and public health issue, rather than through the more common privacy lens.(no content)The law goes into effect on January 1, 2027.\n\n**Political Campaign AI Disclosure (SB 1571):**\nThe legislation mandates disclosure of AI-generated content in campaign materials, imposes fines for non-compliance. Senate Bill 1571, which became law earlier this year, allows the Oregon Secretary of State, or the Attorney General in instances involving candidates for Secretary of State, to seek a court injunction blocking the use of campaign materials that use AI and don't include a disclosure.\n\n**Note:** I did not find a specific AI policy issued by the Oregon Supreme Court itself for use by judges or court personnel. Here's what I discovered:\n\n## Kansas Supreme Court AI Committee\n\nThe 21-member Ad Hoc Artificial Intelligence Committee was created by Supreme Court Administrative Order 2025-CM-017 in February 2025. Its members represent different roles in the Kansas court system and include representatives from the legal and legal-technology communities.\n\n## Kansas Court AI Guidelines\n\nAt the statewide level, Kansas does not yet have comprehensive AI rules issued by the Supreme Court. However, there has been local action:\n\nShawnee County has implemented District Court rule 3.125 Pleadings Using Generative AI. This rule states that if any portion of a pleading or filing was drafted using AI a litigant must (1) verify accuracy and (2) disclose to the court and opposing parties at time of filing that document contains AI content along with a certification that the filing was checked for accuracy. A violation of this rule may result in sanctions.\n\n## State Government AI Policy\n\nSeparately from the courts, the State of Kansas developed a policy to outline acceptable use of generative AI for Executive Branch agencies, serving as the primary governing document for usage of generative AI technology.\n\nThe Kansas Supreme Court appears to be in the early stages of developing comprehensive AI guidance through its newly formed Ad Hoc Committee, while some local courts have already implemented specific rules for AI disclosure requirements."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"47857e9f-0c89-4e5e-b445-9b1628c697c3"}

Here's what I found:\n\n## Kentucky AI Legislation and Policy\n\nOn March 24, 2025, Governor Andy Beshear signed Kentucky Senate Bill 4 (SB 4) into law, with the legislation passing with overwhelming bipartisan support—30-3 in the Senate and 86-10 in the House.(no content)Kentucky has enacted what may be the most comprehensive state-level AI governance law to date.\n\n### Key Provisions of Kentucky's AI Law\n\nThe law, carried by Sen. Amanda Mays Bledsoe, sets standards for how state agencies can use AI and how to report that use to an oversight committee.(no content)Before using high-risk AI, agencies must design and implement protocols to identify and mitigate bias or potential harm.(no content)Any governmental body must disclose the use of generative AI when rendering and informing decisions related to citizens and producing output accessible to the public, providing options to appeal consequential decisions involving AI.\n\n### State Government AI Policy\n\nState agencies may not use high-risk artificial intelligence systems, and an AI tool may be used to generate information that will be an input to an overall process, but no consequential decision shall be rendered without human review.\n\n### Court Transcription Pilot Project\n\nKentucky's General Assembly has introduced House Bill 498, which seeks to establish a pilot project that could reshape how court proceedings are documented and managed across the state, introduced on February 21, 2025.\n\nThe search results do not reveal a specific \"Kentucky Supreme Court artificial intelligence policy\" as a standalone judicial directive, but rather show that Kentucky has implemented comprehensive AI regulation at the state government level that would apply to courts as government agencies."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"058151ff-03e2-40af-b229-843cc6fb79d4"}

Here are the key findings:\n\n## Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission AI Guidelines\n\nThe Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission published Generative Artificial Intelligence Guidelines in October 2025.(no content)While expressly non-binding, the guidelines illuminate the Louisiana Supreme Court's expectations and offer practical guidance to judges regarding the use of AI.\n\n### Key Provisions\n\nThe guidelines make clear that judges must not delegate to AI the evaluation of briefs, the assessment of evidence, or the resolution of legal questions. This reflects a core principle that judicial judgment cannot be replaced by artificial intelligence.\n\n### Development Team\n\nSchlegel, four other judges and a computer science professor developed guidelines for responsible AI use by the judiciary. Judge Scott Schlegel chairs the Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission and was instrumental in creating these guidelines.\n\n### Context\n\nJudge Scott Schlegel of the Louisiana 5th Circuit Court of Appeal has said when judges misuse generative artificial intelligence, it's not just a problem but a potential crisis.(no content)\"When judges do it, it becomes the law. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01XfhbkT6MsW5JMRWHB9snC3","type":"tool_result","content":"\t\t<meta name=\"follow.it-verification-code-L1ZveXkxM29vVmVzNzVnSVpzaEx4T3F6RkFTVXNUdlhQMUdzQnczOGVza05SbEVEem16UTMyUThHN2Jtd1Q4NnB5MTdOMXlxdjB4TVExOTJGOU5QQkFhTzh3bDdDNjYwem00eVhsOGQ4NEJScG5OVUFWZ3lLSzYzMTJjYno4eFJ8QnluZnlrSnZjVlF6Y0R6ZFByRUNCTGdvTk8rN1hZVjBRTUhTNmw1Y2w4cz0=\" content=\"DrThfkqmEbdFeWdyCVjX\"/><meta property=\"og:image:secure_url\" content=\"https://lalegalethics.org/wp-content/uploads/5bcb4e5d-53f0-409d-9bf3-1e1fbdf88c84.png\" data-id=\"sfsi\"><meta property=\"twitter:card\" content=\"summary_large_image\" data-id=\"sfsi\"><meta property=\"twitter:image\" content=\"https://lalegalethics.org/wp-content/uploads/5bcb4e5d-53f0-409d-9bf3-1e1fbdf88c84.png\" data-id=\"sfsi\"><meta property=\"og:image:type\" content=\"\" data-id=\"sfsi\" /><meta property=\"og:image:width\" content=\"1024\" data-id=\"sfsi\" /><meta property=\"og:image:height\" content=\"1024\" data-id=\"sfsi\" /><meta property=\"og:url\" content=\"https://lalegalethics.org/judges-using-genai-louisiana-supreme-court-technology-commission-issues-ai-guidelines-for-judges/\" data-id=\"sfsi\" /><meta property=\"og:description\" content=\"\nThe Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission published Generative Artificial Intelligence Guidelines in October 2025. While expressly non-binding, the guidelines illuminate the Louisiana Supreme Court's expectations and offer practical guidance to judges using this powerful and rapidly-developing technology. \nWe often talk about keeping a 'human in the loop' — a concept embodied in ABA Formal Opinion 512, meaning that a human being, not the machine, must review, verify, and ultimately own every AI-assisted output. The Commission's central concern is exactly that: keeping the human in the loop, with the judge as the decision-maker. In other words, AI is a tool for judges, not a substitute for judicial judgment. The guidelines make clear that judges must not delegate to AI the evaluation of briefs, the assessment of evidence, or the resolution of legal questions. A judge may use AI to identify relevant cases or legal principles, to summarize lengthy documents, or to generate a first draft of a routine order after the judge has independently reached a decision. But the final judgment (the legal reasoning, the weighing of facts, the exercise of judicial discretion) must remain the judge's alone. As the Commission put it, AI should function akin to a law clerk. The analogy holds equally for lawyers: ABA Formal Opinion 512 and the New York State Bar Association have both framed AI output as something to be supervised the way a senior lawyer supervises a junior associate — with active review, verification, and ultimate accountability resting with the human professional.\nThe Commission drew a distinction between permissible and non-permissible uses. On the permissible side: summarizing legal texts, assisting with document drafting and review, conducting legal research, managing case files, and generating training materials. On the non-permissible side: autonomous decision-making, providing legal analysis without independent human verification, and processing sensitive information without robust safeguards. The Commission specifically flagged that generic AI models — those not trained on legal data — are prone to hallucination, lack adequate understanding of caselaw, and can produce contextually misinterpreted or biased output. For that reason, the guidelines caution strongly against using free large language models such as the free versions of ChatGPT or Google AI Studio for any judicial work, recommending instead enterprise-level tools with strong security, privacy, and compliance features.\nJudges who recognize AI's utility want to know how to use it ethically. These guidelines from Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission provide a thorough, meaningful answer.\n\" data-id=\"sfsi\" /><meta property=\"og:title\" content=\"Judges Using GenAI? Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission Issues AI Guidelines for Judges\" data-id=\"sfsi\" /><link rel=\"icon\" href=\"https://lalegalethics.org/wp-content/uploads/cropped-Pelican-Without-Circle-BW-JPEG-32x32.jpg\" sizes=\"32x32\" />\n\t\t\t\t\t\t<h1 class=\"entry-title\">Judges Using GenAI? Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission Issues AI Guidelines for Judges</h1>\t\t\t\t\t</div>\n\t\t\t<a href=\"https://lalegalethics.org/judges-using-genai-louisiana-supreme-court-technology-commission-issues-ai-guidelines-for-judges/\">\n\t\t\t<h2 class=\"entry-title\"><a href=\"https://lalegalethics.org/judges-using-genai-louisiana-supreme-court-technology-commission-issues-ai-guidelines-for-judges/\" rel=\"bookmark\">Judges Using GenAI? Permissible uses include summarizing legal texts, assisting with document drafting and review, conducting legal research, managing case files, and generating training materials. Non-permissible uses include autonomous decision-making, providing legal analysis without independent human verification, and processing sensitive information without safeguards. Guidelines caution against using free large language models like ChatGPT or Google AI Studio for judicial work, recommending enterprise-level tools with strong security, privacy, and compliance features.\",\n  1396→      \"verbatim_key_language\": \"AI is a tool for judges, not a substitute for judicial judgment. The guidelines make clear that judges must not delegate to AI the evaluation of briefs, the assessment of evidence, or the resolution of legal questions.\",\n  1397→      \"last_verified\": \"2026-04-20\",\n  1398→      \"provenance\": \"agent discovery 2026-04-20; Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission Generative AI Guidelines published October 2025\"\n  1399→    }\n  1400→  ]\n  1401→}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"8cede479-82c4-47f8-8467-b6cca20c0862"}

Here's what the search revealed:\n\n## Delaware Supreme Court AI Policy\n\nThe Delaware Supreme Court adopted an interim policy in October 2024 to guide judicial officers and court personnel in the use of generative artificial intelligence. The interim policy neither prohibits nor requires the use of generative AI.\n\n### Key Policy Elements\n\nThe Delaware policy includes several important guidelines:\n\n1. **User Responsibility**: Delaware mandates that any judicial officer using generative AI remains responsible for the output and must ensure AI-derived content is accurate and aligns with their responsibilities.\n\n2. **Informed Use**: The policy requires users to be well-versed in generative AI's capabilities and limitations before applying it in a judicial context, with training in the technical capabilities and limitations of approved GenAI before use.\n\n3. **Decision-Making Limits**: Courts emphasize that AI should not influence judicial decisions or replace human judgment.\n\n4. **Approved Tools**: The policy creates a category of \"Approved GenAI,\" which includes tools that have been approved by the court system's administrative office.\n\n5. **Compliance**: AI use must conform to existing laws and policies in Delaware's judicial branch.\n\nThe DCLT (Delaware Commission on Law and Technology) is to monitor how the interim policy is implemented and report any proposed changes that may come to light as GenAI usage expands in the Delaware courts."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"b58b21a9-115e-4f6d-9e1e-a8fac2a35599"}

This opinion addresses key ethical considerations including:\n\n- **Competence and supervision requirements** - Generative AI tools are not always accurate, requiring careful attention to competence and supervision to avoid false statements of material fact or law to a tribunal\n- **Confidentiality concerns**\n- **Candor to the court obligations**\n- **Supervisory responsibilities** for managing lawyers\n\nThe opinion recommends developing an ethical framework for generative AI use and providing appropriate training for lawyers and nonlawyers, just as with any other resource or tool used in the practice of law.\n\n### Related Missouri Legal Developments\n\nWhile no laws have been enacted in Missouri specifically regulating AI, bills have been introduced in the state legislature requiring disclaimers on political advertisements made or edited with AI.\n\nAdditionally, Missouri House Bill 1462 would establish the \"AI Non-Sentience and Responsibility Act,\" which comprehensively defines artificial intelligence and sets clear legal boundaries for AI systems' status and accountability."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"079c9653-82c9-4e2e-88bb-97bcd95c0f75"}

The search results cover both the Utah Judicial Council's internal AI policy for courts AND the Utah state legislative AI policy. Let me provide you with a complete summary:\n\n## Search Results Summary\n\nThe search results reveal two distinct Utah AI policies from 2024-2025:\n\n### 1. **Utah Judicial Council's Interim Rules on Generative AI (October 2023)**\n\nBeginning October 25, 2023 the Judicial Council implemented these interim rules regarding the use of generative AI tools for court-related work or on court-owned devices.\n\nKey provisions include:\n- Judges and court employees should recognize the limitations of generative AI and may not rely solely on AI-generated content.\n- The Interim Rules require that any court personnel that use generative AI tools must first complete court-approved training prior to use, and all employees must disclose any use of generative AI tools to judicial officers.\n- The Interim Rules also warn that generative AI tools have been known to produce outputs that inadvertently promote stereotypes, reinforce prejudices, or exhibit unfair biases.\n\n### 2. **Utah Artificial Intelligence Policy Act (UAIPA) - Legislative Policy**\n\nIn March 2024, Utah became the first state to enact legislation specifically targeting generative AI (Gen AI). The Utah Artificial Intelligence Policy Act (UAIPA), which took effect on May 1, 2024, imposed certain disclosure obligations on entities using Gen AI tools to interact with their customers.\n\n**2025 Amendments:**\nUtah recently passed three AI laws (HB 452, SB 226 and SB 332), all of which became effective on May 7, 2025, and either amend or expand the scope of the UAIPA.\n\nKey features:\n- Disclosure is only required if the use of generative AI constitutes a \"high-risk artificial intelligence interaction,\" which must be provided verbally at the start of a verbal conversation and in writing before the start of a written interaction.\n- SB 332 extends the duration of the AIPA, which was scheduled to sunset on May 7, 2025, until July 1, 2027.\n- Violation of the law may result in administrative fines up to $2,500 per violation and a court action by the Utah Division of Consumer Protection."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"ba4bbac4-6226-439b-808e-02a638a0acbb"}

Here's what the search revealed:\n\n## Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts\n\nChief Justice Mark Recktenwald established a Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts in April 2024 chaired by Supreme Court Justice Vladimir Devens and First Circuit Court Judge John Tonaki. \n\n## Final Report (December 2025)\n\nIn December 2025, a panel of judges and other legal experts presented a long-awaited report on AI and the court system to acting Chief Justice Sabrina McKenna. \n\nThe report addresses several key areas:\n\n- **AI Applications**: The report explores numerous ways that legal professionals can use AI tools with training and human oversight to process documents, create court transcripts and minutes, and provide information to non-lawyers representing themselves in litigation and people who don't speak English.\n\n- **Guardrails**: Guardrails must be considered with the implementation of any future AI technology relating to the Judiciary.\n\n- **Committee Oversight**: The report recommends the formation of a committee to oversee the development and management of AI technology ensuring consistency with the Judiciary's mission and values.\n\n- **Human Oversight**: Before implementing or purchasing any new AI technology, the committee should become well-informed as to how the technology will be used, its risks, and the vendor's terms of use.\n\n## Key Finding\n\nDespite lawyers citing fake, AI-generated citations in court documents, the new report finds existing rules provide adequate safeguards.\n\nThe full report is available on the Hawaii State Courts website."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dc995ec2-a089-4a2c-b95a-2306569a210a","uuid":"00017d23-a121-43ca-93fc-df2ba470a968"}
